abridged.content:
Cardiac output - the flow that actually perfuses organs

Cardiac output is the volume of blood ejected by the heart each minute. It is the core flow variable behind organ perfusion. Blood pressure matters, but pressure is not the same as flow. A patient can have an acceptable MAP because SVR is high while true forward flow is poor.

The formula is CO = HR × SV. Heart rate is beats per minute. Stroke volume is the amount ejected per beat. Typical adult resting CO is about 4-8 L/min, commonly 5-6 L/min. Cardiac index corrects for body surface area, with a common minimum CTICU target of ≥2.2 L/min/m².

Stroke volume is determined by preload, afterload, and contractility. Preload is filling/stretch, afterload is resistance to ejection, and contractility is intrinsic pump force. Excessive heart rate can reduce stroke volume by shortening diastolic filling time.

In CTICU, CO is interpreted through MAP, pulse pressure, vasoactive dose, urine output, lactate, SvO₂, echo, skin perfusion, temperature, and rhythm. The key question is whether the pressure is being achieved with adequate forward flow.

expanded.content:
Cardiac output - the flow that actually perfuses organs

This topic must be understood as a mechanism, not as a list of numbers. In CTICU, the same numerical value can mean different things depending on rhythm, ventricular function, vascular tone, ventilation, oxygen content, temperature, bleeding, surgical context, and the patient’s response over time.

Heart rate

Heart rate determines how many cardiac cycles occur per minute. It can increase cardiac output only while stroke volume is maintained. Excessive rates shorten diastole, reduce ventricular filling, reduce coronary perfusion time, and increase myocardial oxygen demand.

Mechanistically, heart rate affects the relationship between ventricular filling, pressure generation, ejection, venous return, and tissue oxygen delivery. When this variable changes, the arterial waveform, pulse pressure, cardiac index, filling pressures, lactate, urine output, and venous saturation may change before the patient looks obviously unstable.

At the bedside, assess heart rate by looking for concordance. A trustworthy interpretation requires the monitor value, waveform, clinical perfusion, recent trend, and response to therapy to point in the same direction. If the number and patient disagree, validate the equipment and escalate the physiological question rather than treating the number alone.

Stroke volume

Stroke volume is the volume ejected with each beat. It depends on preload, afterload, and contractility. A low stroke volume appears as narrow pulse pressure, weak arterial upstroke, low CI, rising lactate, low SvO₂, and reduced urine output.

Mechanistically, stroke volume affects the relationship between ventricular filling, pressure generation, ejection, venous return, and tissue oxygen delivery. When this variable changes, the arterial waveform, pulse pressure, cardiac index, filling pressures, lactate, urine output, and venous saturation may change before the patient looks obviously unstable.

At the bedside, assess stroke volume by looking for concordance. A trustworthy interpretation requires the monitor value, waveform, clinical perfusion, recent trend, and response to therapy to point in the same direction. If the number and patient disagree, validate the equipment and escalate the physiological question rather than treating the number alone.

Preload

Preload is the filling/stretch available before contraction. It supports stroke volume through the Frank-Starling mechanism, but only until the ventricle reaches the flat part of the curve.

Mechanistically, preload affects the relationship between ventricular filling, pressure generation, ejection, venous return, and tissue oxygen delivery. When this variable changes, the arterial waveform, pulse pressure, cardiac index, filling pressures, lactate, urine output, and venous saturation may change before the patient looks obviously unstable.

At the bedside, assess preload by looking for concordance. A trustworthy interpretation requires the monitor value, waveform, clinical perfusion, recent trend, and response to therapy to point in the same direction. If the number and patient disagree, validate the equipment and escalate the physiological question rather than treating the number alone.

Afterload

Afterload is the load against which the ventricle ejects. Excessive afterload can preserve pressure while reducing flow, especially in a weak ventricle.

Mechanistically, afterload affects the relationship between ventricular filling, pressure generation, ejection, venous return, and tissue oxygen delivery. When this variable changes, the arterial waveform, pulse pressure, cardiac index, filling pressures, lactate, urine output, and venous saturation may change before the patient looks obviously unstable.

At the bedside, assess afterload by looking for concordance. A trustworthy interpretation requires the monitor value, waveform, clinical perfusion, recent trend, and response to therapy to point in the same direction. If the number and patient disagree, validate the equipment and escalate the physiological question rather than treating the number alone.

Contractility

Contractility is intrinsic myocardial force generation. It is impaired by stunning, ischaemia, acidosis, hypothermia, and electrolyte problems.

Mechanistically, contractility affects the relationship between ventricular filling, pressure generation, ejection, venous return, and tissue oxygen delivery. When this variable changes, the arterial waveform, pulse pressure, cardiac index, filling pressures, lactate, urine output, and venous saturation may change before the patient looks obviously unstable.

At the bedside, assess contractility by looking for concordance. A trustworthy interpretation requires the monitor value, waveform, clinical perfusion, recent trend, and response to therapy to point in the same direction. If the number and patient disagree, validate the equipment and escalate the physiological question rather than treating the number alone.

Cardiac index

cardiac index is a core component of this topic. It must be interpreted through the CTICU pattern rather than as an isolated number. The clinical meaning depends on pressure, flow, filling, oxygen delivery, oxygen demand, waveform quality, and surgical context.

Mechanistically, cardiac index affects the relationship between ventricular filling, pressure generation, ejection, venous return, and tissue oxygen delivery. When this variable changes, the arterial waveform, pulse pressure, cardiac index, filling pressures, lactate, urine output, and venous saturation may change before the patient looks obviously unstable.

At the bedside, assess cardiac index by looking for concordance. A trustworthy interpretation requires the monitor value, waveform, clinical perfusion, recent trend, and response to therapy to point in the same direction. If the number and patient disagree, validate the equipment and escalate the physiological question rather than treating the number alone.

Pressure-flow separation

pressure-flow separation is a core component of this topic. It must be interpreted through the CTICU pattern rather than as an isolated number. The clinical meaning depends on pressure, flow, filling, oxygen delivery, oxygen demand, waveform quality, and surgical context.

Mechanistically, pressure-flow separation affects the relationship between ventricular filling, pressure generation, ejection, venous return, and tissue oxygen delivery. When this variable changes, the arterial waveform, pulse pressure, cardiac index, filling pressures, lactate, urine output, and venous saturation may change before the patient looks obviously unstable.

At the bedside, assess pressure-flow separation by looking for concordance. A trustworthy interpretation requires the monitor value, waveform, clinical perfusion, recent trend, and response to therapy to point in the same direction. If the number and patient disagree, validate the equipment and escalate the physiological question rather than treating the number alone.

Low cardiac output syndrome

low cardiac output syndrome is a core component of this topic. It must be interpreted through the CTICU pattern rather than as an isolated number. The clinical meaning depends on pressure, flow, filling, oxygen delivery, oxygen demand, waveform quality, and surgical context.

Mechanistically, low cardiac output syndrome affects the relationship between ventricular filling, pressure generation, ejection, venous return, and tissue oxygen delivery. When this variable changes, the arterial waveform, pulse pressure, cardiac index, filling pressures, lactate, urine output, and venous saturation may change before the patient looks obviously unstable.

At the bedside, assess low cardiac output syndrome by looking for concordance. A trustworthy interpretation requires the monitor value, waveform, clinical perfusion, recent trend, and response to therapy to point in the same direction. If the number and patient disagree, validate the equipment and escalate the physiological question rather than treating the number alone.

CTICU bedside integration

Start by confirming the measurement is real: level, zero, waveform quality, sampling site, timing, rhythm, and patient position. Then separate pressure from flow. Ask whether the patient has adequate cardiac output, adequate vascular tone, acceptable filling pressures, tolerable afterload, adequate oxygen content, and acceptable oxygen demand.

Next, decide which mechanism is most likely failing: preload, afterload, contractility, rhythm, obstruction, oxygen content, or oxygen demand. The intervention follows the mechanism. Fluid is for preload-responsive and fluid-tolerant physiology. Vasopressor is for inadequate vascular tone or perfusion pressure. Inotropy is for inadequate pump force. Ventilation and oxygenation changes are haemodynamic interventions when PVR or work of breathing is part of the problem.

Finally, reassess after every intervention. A useful intervention improves flow or oxygen economy: stronger pulse pressure, improved CI, better urine output, falling lactate, improved SvO₂, improved skin perfusion, or reduced vasoactive requirement. A harmful intervention raises filling pressure, worsens oxygenation, worsens RV dilation, increases afterload excessively, or improves MAP while lactate and SvO₂ worsen.

Pattern table

FindingMeaningCTICU action
Rising lactateOxygen delivery-demand mismatch or metabolic stressCheck CO/CI, Hb, SaO₂, temperature, vasoactive dose, and perfusion
Low urine outputLow renal perfusion, venous congestion, AKI, or vasoconstrictionAssess MAP, CI, CVP, fluid balance, renal trend, and congestion
Falling SvO₂Increased extraction from low delivery or high demandAssess flow, Hb, oxygenation, fever, shivering, pain, and WOB

pearls.content:
Clinical Pearls - Cardiac Output and Determinants

Pearl 1 - Treat the mechanism, not the number

Cardiac Output and Determinants becomes clinically useful only when linked to preload, afterload, contractility, rhythm, oxygen delivery, and demand.

Pearl 2 - Trends beat snapshots

A changing trend with worsening lactate, urine output, pulse pressure, or SvO₂ is more important than a single apparently acceptable value.

Pearl 3 - Validate before acting

Check waveform, zero, level, sample site, timing, rhythm, ventilation, and patient position before escalating therapy based on a number.

Pearl 4 - The RV and LV may need different things

LV-focused management can harm the RV if PVR, CVP, and septal shift are ignored.

equipment.content:
Equipment Tie-In - Cardiac Output and Determinants

Arterial line: shows pressure, pulse pressure, upstroke, damping, and response to therapy.

CVP/PAC: helps separate filling pressure, PA pressure, CO/CI, SVR, PVR, and SvO₂ when present.

Echo: identifies chamber size, LV/RV function, septal shift, tamponade, valve problems, and gross filling state.

Ventilator: affects venous return, PVR, oxygenation, PaCO₂, pH, and work of breathing.

ABG/POCT: provides lactate, pH, PaCO₂, PaO₂, Hb, potassium, ionised calcium, and other variables that change haemodynamics.

targets.content:
Targets and Numbers - Cardiac Output and Determinants

Targets are pattern-based rather than isolated. Common CTICU reference points include MAP 65-85 mmHg, CI ≥2.2 L/min/m², urine output ≥0.5 mL/kg/hr, lactate <2 mmol/L or falling, SvO₂ 60-80% or ≥65-70% when appropriate, CVP often 5-12 mmHg depending context, and PAWP often 8-15 mmHg if valid. Patient type, surgical procedure, RV risk, bleeding risk, valve repair, aortic surgery, and chronic hypertension may shift targets.

tables.heading: Core values and interpretation
rows: Must be matched with CI, lactate, UO, and SvO₂
Low with poor perfusion suggests low-output physiology
High with shock suggests RV/obstructive/congestive physiology
High with hypoxia suggests congestion or LV dysfunction
Rising suggests delivery-demand mismatch or severe stress
Low in low flow, venous congestion, AKI, or vasoconstriction

tables.heading: CTICU pattern recognition
rows: Warm, bounding pulse, low diastolic pressure
Narrow pulse pressure, cool, low UO

quizQuestions.question: What is the safest way to interpret Cardiac Output and Determinants in CTICU?
options: As part of a full haemodynamic pattern
By treating every abnormal value with fluid
quizQuestions.explanation: Cardiac Output and Determinants must be interpreted with pressure, flow, waveform quality, rhythm, oxygen delivery, demand, and clinical response.

quizQuestions.question: A patient has conflicting data related to Cardiac Output and Determinants. What should happen first?
options: Validate the measurement and correlate with the clinical picture
Assume the number is correct and treat immediately
Give a large fluid bolus automatically
quizQuestions.explanation: Measurement validity and clinical correlation come first because transducer error, sampling error, damping, rhythm, ventilation, and patient position can all change interpretation.

quizQuestions.question: Why is isolated interpretation of Cardiac Output and Determinants dangerous after cardiac surgery?
options: Because preload, afterload, contractility, rhythm, ventilation, oxygen content, and demand interact
Because CTICU patients do not need haemodynamic monitoring
Because normal MAP always proves normal flow
Because all post-op cardiac patients have identical physiology
quizQuestions.explanation: After cardiac surgery the haemodynamic system is interactive. A single value can reflect multiple mechanisms and can be misleading unless interpreted with the full CTICU pattern.
